prompt_md: When implementing `operator<<` for `IntStack` that prints `[a, b, c]`, the loop should iterate how many times?

options.a: Always `MAX_SIZE` (8) times, so all slots are visible.
options.b: `m_count` times, because only that many elements contain valid data.
options.c: `MAX_SIZE - m_count` times, to skip the used portion.
options.d: Once, printing the top element only.

explanation_md: Only the first `m_count` elements of the internal array hold valid data. Iterating beyond that prints garbage from uninitialized memory. The stream operator must always use `m_count` as its loop bound.
Three pushes add 10, 20, 30 to the stack (bottom to top). One prefix `--` pops the top element (30). The remaining stack printed bottom-to-top is `[10, 20]`.

prompt_md: The copy constructor for `IntStack` must copy which members?

options.a: Only `m_count`, because the array elements are recomputed on access.
options.c: Only `m_data` — `m_count` is always recomputed from the array.
options.d: Neither — the default memberwise copy is always correct for arrays.

explanation_md: A correct copy must replicate both the count and the data. Copying only `m_count` leaves `m_data` uninitialized in the copy. Copying only `m_data` leaves `m_count` as 0 so the copy appears empty. The compiler's default memberwise copy would actually work here (it copies fixed arrays element-by-element), but writing it explicitly is the point of the exercise.

prompt_md: Why should `operator bool()` on `IntStack` be declared `explicit`?

options.b: Without `explicit`, the stack could silently convert to `int` in arithmetic expressions via the `bool → int` promotion chain.
options.c: `explicit` is required on all `operator bool` declarations by the standard.
options.d: Without `explicit`, the operator cannot be used in `if` conditions.

explanation_md: Without `explicit`, an `IntStack` could be used wherever an `int` is expected: `int n = stack;` would compile and give 0 or 1. `explicit` blocks implicit conversions but still allows the operator in boolean contexts (`if`, `while`, `?:`), which is exactly where it is meant to be used.
